categories.name: Geopolitical Risk
categories.description: Risk arising from political instability, trade restrictions, sanctions, and diplomatic tensions that can disrupt mineral supply chains.

indicators.name: Trade Policy Restrictiveness
indicators.description: Presence and severity of export bans, tariffs, quotas, or strategic stockpiling policies affecting mineral exports.

indicators.name: Sanctions Exposure
indicators.description: Risk of economic or trade sanctions being imposed on producing country, affecting mineral exports.

indicators.name: Political Stability Index
indicators.description: World Bank governance indicator measuring likelihood of political instability or politically motivated violence.

indicators.name: Diplomatic Relations
indicators.description: State of bilateral/multilateral relations between producing country and major consuming nations.

indicators.name: Armed Conflict Risk
indicators.description: Likelihood and impact of armed conflict or civil unrest disrupting mining operations.

categories.name: Environmental Risk
categories.description: Risk arising from environmental degradation, water scarcity, climate vulnerability, and biodiversity impact that can constrain mining operations.

indicators.name: Water Stress Level
indicators.description: World Resources Institute water stress index for mining regions. High water stress can limit processing capacity.

indicators.name: Biodiversity Impact
indicators.description: Proximity of mining operations to biodiversity hotspots and protected areas. IUCN Red List overlap.

indicators.name: Carbon Footprint Intensity
indicators.description: GHG emissions per ton of mineral produced. Relevant for Scope 3 reporting and carbon border adjustments.

indicators.name: Climate Vulnerability
indicators.description: ND-GAIN Index score for producing country's vulnerability to climate change impacts on mining operations.

indicators.name: Tailings Management Risk
indicators.description: Assessment of tailings dam safety and environmental liability based on global tailings standard compliance.

categories.name: Regulatory Risk
categories.description: Risk arising from changes in mining codes, tax policies, environmental regulations, and permitting processes that can increase costs or delay operations.

indicators.name: Mining Code Stability
indicators.description: Frequency and impact of changes to mining legislation, royalty rates, or fiscal terms.

indicators.name: Permitting Timeline
indicators.description: Average time from exploration license to production permit. Longer timelines increase development risk.

indicators.name: Tax & Royalty Policy
indicators.description: Effective tax and royalty burden on mining operations, including recent and proposed changes.

indicators.name: Local Content Requirements
indicators.description: Mandates for local processing, hiring, or procurement that may affect operational flexibility and costs.

indicators.name: ESG Regulatory Burden
indicators.description: Number and complexity of ESG-related regulations (EU CSRD, SEC climate disclosure, local equivalents).

categories.name: Infrastructure Risk
categories.description: Risk arising from inadequate transport, energy, water, and digital infrastructure that can limit mining and processing capacity.

indicators.name: Power Grid Reliability
indicators.description: Electricity supply reliability (SAIDI index) and cost for mining operations. Load shedding frequency in producing regions.

indicators.name: Transport Infrastructure
indicators.description: Quality of road, rail, and port infrastructure serving mining regions. Logistics cost per ton-km.

indicators.name: Water Access & Treatment
indicators.description: Availability of process water and capacity for water treatment and recycling at mine sites.

indicators.name: Digital Connectivity
indicators.description: Internet and telecommunications infrastructure quality for remote monitoring and autonomous mining operations.

categories.name: Labor Risk
categories.description: Risk arising from labor disputes, skill shortages, safety incidents, and labor rights concerns that can disrupt operations.

indicators.name: Skilled Labor Availability
indicators.description: Availability of mining engineers, geologists, and skilled operators in producing regions.

indicators.name: Labor Relations Climate
indicators.description: Frequency and severity of strikes, union disputes, and collective bargaining outcomes.

indicators.name: Health & Safety Record
indicators.description: Fatalities and lost-time injury frequency rates (LTIFR) relative to global industry benchmarks.

indicators.name: Child Labor Risk
indicators.description: Prevalence of child labor in artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM) in producing regions. US DoL listings.

indicators.name: Community Relations
indicators.description: Social license to operate strength. History of community opposition, protests, or blockades at mine sites.

categories.name: Market Concentration Risk
categories.description: Risk arising from supply concentration in few countries, companies, or trade routes. Measured using the Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI).

indicators.name: Country-Level HHI
indicators.description: Herfindahl-Hirschman Index measuring production concentration across countries. HHI > 2,500 = highly concentrated.

indicators.name: Company-Level HHI
indicators.description: Concentration of production among top mining companies. Higher HHI = more supply chain vulnerability.

indicators.name: Processing Concentration
indicators.description: Share of mineral processing/refining controlled by top 3 countries. Especially relevant for rare earths.

indicators.name: Trade Route Dependency
indicators.description: Reliance on specific maritime chokepoints (Strait of Malacca, Suez Canal, Panama Canal) for mineral shipments.

indicators.name: Substitution Difficulty
indicators.description: Availability and economic viability of substitute minerals or recycling sources. Higher = greater supply chain rigidity.

riskLevels.description: Supply chain is highly diversified with minimal disruption risk.
Some concentration exists but multiple supply sources are available.
Moderate concentration risk. Monitor political and environmental developments.
Significant concentration risk. Diversification strategies recommended.
Extreme supply chain vulnerability. Active risk mitigation required.
